narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 55.5% chance that you would not pass—crossing the threshold we use to make such decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, LSAT score of 37, Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles of 3 and 1 respectively, middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. Among students who passed the bar, the average undergraduate GPA is 3.24 and LSAT around 37.27, while successful full-time students make up over 93% of passers, so your profile aligns in some expected ways but stands out in others. The model's decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 decile ranking of 1, which was the strongest factor pushing the prediction toward bar failure, as it signals performance well below what we've seen in those who succeed. Your LSAT score played the next biggest role, but in a positive way, pulling the prediction toward passing since it was close to the average for bar passers. Coming in third was your undergraduate GPA, which also nudged things slightly toward success compared to typical profiles, though not enough to offset the top concern. Overall, your Year 3 standing was the dominant influence, far outweighing these strengths and differing sharply from accepted students whose later-year deciles average nearly 6. While your middle income and full-time plans fit common patterns among passers, they didn't sway the outcome much here. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven forecast based on patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps targeting a higher LSAT for an even stronger edge, and reapplying once you've strengthened those key academic markers. Many students in similar spots have improved their profiles and succeeded, so focus on actionable steps like academic advising or targeted study. You're capable of turning this around with the right preparation.
